comment.body:
1) MM net short skew = net short OTM puts, long OTM calls (positive speed = shorter downside gamma, longer upside gamma)
A positioning like this creates a net negative charm flow and positive vanna flow.
but most importantly: the charm convexity (color) is positive!!
so there will be strikes where gamma will increase as time passes, creating local magnets.
the analyst must spot out, which one has the most weight, scrutinizing speed

comment.body:
because, imagine you have a MM local short fly:
you are just below the center, so you have positive speed, short skew flow.
charm is negative = net buyback flow from delta decay,
color is postiive = central gamma is increasing
you need to take the gamma convexity (speed) between OTM shorter gamma and the central long gamma, find the centroid, where the pressure on the potentially upward grinding momentum eases (bcs long gamma forces dealers to sell against the momentum, but the convexity has a peak, from there it eases)

comment.body:
veta is vega decay.
long veta = dealer is longer vega by the time until it reaches zero exposure buying back less and less vega,
short veta = opposite.

why? dealers hedge vol exposure too, but not in every timeframe, and not always... its the effect that matters.
vega needs 4 weeks or more in terms of maturity to have an effect on the dealer flow.
but look...
vega can be hedged only by options, bcs futures have no vega..
but it is vanna that matters the most from vega as its first derivative.

comment.body:
veta is vega decay.
long veta = dealer is longer vega by the time until it reaches zero exposure buying back less and less vega,
short veta = opposite.

why? dealers hedge vol exposure too, but not in every timeframe, and not always... its the effect that matters.
vega needs 4 weeks or more in terms of maturity to have an effect on the dealer flow.
but look...
vega can be hedged only by options, bcs futures have no vega..
but it is vanna that matters the most from vega as its first derivative.

comment.body:
technical indicators are not useful during oscillating trend.
literally no exact answer... in the case of SPX, due to the frequency of the option expos it is more easier to foresee a choppy trend... monitoring macro flows like rotation, etc.
bcs if index vol is sold, beta goes down to, bcs the moves of the baskets annihilate each other.
one can meassure the RoC of the beta to see how it evolves... + take the volatility of the curve to see what counts as "healthy" momentum shift (rough estimation)... also one can see if the mean reverting tendecy is growing or not...

but at the end of the day, there is no free lunch, market cannot be solved.
we only have distribution, certain zones derived from momentum and positioning that reveals us with higher probability, what the market wants. 
but still not 100%, only 'higher' probability
